text.content:
How the Investigation Works

Phase 6 is a node-based investigation. No single clue is required. Any two primary nodes reveal V. Korre. Any three primary nodes reveal the eleventh-bell transfer. The climax is reachable even if the party fails one node.

The six nodes

  1. Tellan Verth — tavern witness (Foreign Quarter, the Hand of Coals).
  2. The Brass Crow counting-house — Tinker's Way, Foreign Quarter.
  3. Velash Manuscripts & Bindings — Garden Quarter.
  4. Edrik Vone's private courier archive — High Quay.
  5. Kestrel & Reed, Foreign Letters — Hawkstone Way.
  6. V. Korre's monthly address — rotating; current address recoverable from any of the above.

Clue redundancy

ClueRecovered from
Haskur is in GreyhawkVerth, Brass Crow registry, Vone's irregular ledger
Haskur uses alias H. V. AndrenVerth, Brass Crow registry, Vone's irregular ledger
The younger man existsVerth, Brass Crow surveillance, Korre's address
V. Korre is the Greyhawk handlerBrass Crow side mark + Vone's irregular ledger (any 2 of: Brass Crow, Vone, K&R)
Eleventh-bell transferBrass Crow time notation + Kestrel & Reed packet slip + Vone's pending pickup (any 3 of the 6 nodes)
The Seventh StairIren Velash reading, the Ashen Itinerary, Korre's apartment route notation, the cistern route mark

Clock effects

Each visible entry into a primary node advances the Haskur Trail Clock by 1. Surveillance and Tellan Verth do not advance the Trail Clock. Each in-city day with any visible action advances the Greyhawk Heat Clock by 1. See Phase 6 — Clocks & Carry-Forward.

What success looks like

The party should feel clever when they connect the nodes — not when they roll well. Reward triangulation: if the party brings two pieces of evidence to a single NPC, give them more than each piece alone would have given. Iren in particular reads better when she has more to read.

pages.name: Node 1 — Tellan Verth (the Hand of Coals)

text.content:
Node 1 — Tellan Verth (the Hand of Coals)

See Tellan's full dossier under Phase 6 — Greyhawk NPCs. This page covers the scene at the tavern.

Read aloud — the Hand of Coals

The Hand of Coals sits four streets east of the Brass Crow, on a dog-leg corner where a narrow alley spills into a wider street. The sign is a hand cradling embers, the paint flaking. The door is propped open against the heat of the kitchen.

Inside, the room is panelled in pitch-pine that has darkened with two generations of smoke. Three small tables, a bench along the back wall, a stairwell up to rented rooms. The barkeep does not look up.

At the back-left bench, beside the chimney, sits an old man in clean brown wool. A brass-headed cane is propped against his knee. He is not drinking. He is staring at the door.

When you sit down opposite him, he does not look up. After a moment he says, quietly, “Are you the ones?”

How the conversation goes

If the party indicates — even obliquely — that they were sent by Caelith Dunivar, Verth closes his eyes for a moment and says: “I have been waiting for you.”

If the party does not signal Caelith, Verth is still cooperative but more cautious. Persuasion DC 13 opens him up; Insight DC 14 reads his shame; each detail beyond the first three requires a follow-up question, not a re-roll.

What Verth confirms (in order, with prompting)

  1. The man at the Brass Crow was Haskur Vandrell.
  2. Haskur entered at the third bell after noon, on Tinker's Way, two streets north of the main square.
  3. The desk clerk repeated the alias: H. V. Andren.
  4. The younger man was already inside; Verth thinks he came in from the back alley.
  5. The younger man wore grey-and-bone clothing, unfamiliar cut.
  6. The younger man's gait was wrong: small constant corrections, as if walking somewhere narrower.
  7. He watched the lintel and the street's reflection, not people.
  8. Haskur listened more than he spoke. The younger man did most of the talking.
  9. Verth could not follow them out. He apologises for this, twice.

The complication

One other person in the tavern is listening. He is a thin man at the bar, drinking water; he leaves before the party does. Passive Perception 14 notices him. Insight 13 reads his attention.

This is Tassel, the Brass Crow runner. If the party does not catch him — or shadow him — he reports the conversation to the desk clerk by the next bell, and the Haskur Trail Clock advances by 1.

If the party does catch and follow Tassel, they get an early clue: he runs a circuit (cobbler shop on Tinker's Way, Brass Crow back door, two more drops, then a courier office on Hawkstone Way). The courier office is Kestrel & Reed. Following Tassel quietly compresses the investigation by half a day.

What Verth wants in return

  • To have the matter taken off his hands. He hands the party a folded statement (Handout 2) with shaking hands.
  • To be reassured that he was not responsible for what may have come of his failure to follow.
  • To meet again only if necessary. He is not a recurring NPC; he is a witness. He returns to the Hand of Coals every evening regardless.

If protected

If the party arranges quiet protection for Verth (Caelith's seal, a private letter to a Foreign Quarter advocate, a one-week paid bodyguard), he survives the phase. If they do not, and the Haskur Trail Clock reaches 4 or higher, Verth is killed in his rooms above the Hand of Coals on the night of the eleventh bell. The party will find him in the morning if they go looking. This is the only NPC death the GM should consider authorial.

Node 2 — The Brass Crow Counting-House

Description

Foreign Quarter, two streets north of the main square, on Tinker's Way. A small, three-storey Aerdy-aligned counting-house specialising in foreign and emigrant clients. Brass-bound doors. A brass crow above the lintel. Four staff, a registry shelf, a strong drawer. It is not a bank. It is a place where money becomes correspondence and correspondence becomes money.

Keyed location

#RoomNotes
1Front ledger hallTwo trestle tables, the registry shelf, the desk clerk's station. Open during business hours. The brass crow sign is visible from outside.
2Waiting benchesTwo narrow benches against the side wall, for clients waiting their turn.
3Outer deskWenna Roost's station. Registry book is here when in use; otherwise on the registry shelf.
4Registry shelfThe current and the previous quarter's registries, in a low locked cabinet. Lock DC 14. The Andren entry is in the current registry.
5Private consultation roomMarked clients are admitted here after the desk clerk checks the lintel notch. Korre uses this room when she visits in person.
6Strong drawerA locked iron drawer behind the proprietor's desk. Lock DC 17. Holds two months of foreign-correspondent payment receipts and the marked-client list.
7Rear counting officeAerel Mossen's office. Has an inner door to the consultation room. Aerel keeps a small ledger here that is NOT the registry.
8Back alley exitSteps down to a service passage that connects to the Old Stonecistern's drainage. Used by Tassel and by the younger man.
9Roof / upper window lineThree storeys, slate roof. The third floor has a guest room rented by H. V. Andren. The window opens onto the alley side.

Approach options

Each approach is viable. Each has a cost.

Social approach

The party walks in as travelling merchants. Wenna Roost handles them at the desk. Persuasion DC 13 gets them basic registry access (current week only). DC 16 gets the previous month. Aerel Mossen appears if questions become specific; Persuasion DC 17 or appropriate leverage opens his cooperation.

Cost: advances the Haskur Trail Clock by 1. The desk clerk may be dismissed if questioned visibly — protect Wenna or lose her.

Surveillance approach

The party watches the door from across Tinker's Way. The cobbler's upper room rents for 2 sp a day. They observe arrivals, departures, lintel checks, and the runner's circuit.

Cost: time only. Does not advance the Trail Clock unless the party is seen watching (Stealth/Deception DC 13 to remain unobtrusive).

Burglary approach

Entry is possible through the alley back door (DC 16 lock, DC 14 to scout the timing of Tassel's runs), through the third-floor window from the cobbler's roof (DC 15 climb, DC 14 latch), or through the cellar service drain from the Old Stonecistern side (this is reverse access; only viable late in the phase).

Cost: advances the Trail Clock by 2 if any sign of entry is left. Advances by 1 if entry is clean. Triggers Heat Clock advancement if the City Watch is summoned.

Official approach

The party shows Yeomanry papers or a Greyhawk advocate's writ. Aerel cooperates publicly — and warns Korre privately within the hour.

Cost: advances the Trail Clock by 2 and the Heat Clock by 1. Korre changes address that night.

Clues recoverable from the registry / strong drawer

  • H. V. Andren appears in the current registry, third entry of last week. The handwriting is competent but not Aerdi.
  • Beside the entry is a copied side mark: a small symbol matching the Korre channel — a single vertical stroke crossed at three-quarter height by a short horizontal. This is the foreign-correspondent channel mark.
  • A payment note in the strong drawer ties Andren's account to a sender at Kestrel & Reed, Foreign Letters.
  • A time notation on the back of the payment note: “11th bell, rain door.”
  • A phrase written in the proprietor's hand on a separate slip: “second page not burned.” Aerel does not know what this means; he was paid extra to file the slip without filing it.

Staff behaviour during entry

  • Wenna Roost — helpful if treated kindly; will straighten the inkwell when frightened.
  • Aerel Mossen — cooperative once shown leverage; will warn Korre privately if Yeomanry papers appear.
  • Tassel — on circuit; runs to Hawkstone Way (Kestrel & Reed) every six hours.

If the party enters on day one

Caelith warned them not to. Advance the Haskur Trail Clock by 1 immediately and play out Korre's reaction by the second day: she changes the lintel-check signal, and the desk clerk reads a slightly different notch on the lintel from then forward. Any party member who returns and is not informed of the change will not be admitted to the consultation room.

Node 3 — Velash Manuscripts & Bindings

Garden Quarter, on a quiet street whose stones are older than the building. The shop sign is a small black book on a dark green ground. The shutters are usually half-closed.

Keyed location

#RoomNotes
1Front shelf roomThree walls of low shelves, stacked with bound and unbound manuscripts, a long reading table at the centre. Iren spends most of her day here.
2Reading tableSingle chair, a brass reading lamp, a small inkwell. The party reads under Iren's eye if she has accepted the commission.
3Binding benchAt the back left. Threads, awls, a hand-press, jars of paste. Iren works here when she has commissions.
4Locked paper pressHolds the older or rarer items, including the obsolete mining-route glossary that contains the “Seventh Stair” reference. Lock DC 16.
5Upstairs living roomIren's living quarters. Off limits to clients; entered by trusted patrons only. A small kitchen, two bookshelves, a window onto the garden.
6Rear stair to alleyIren's private exit. Locked from inside. Used in case of fire or unwelcome visitors.

Entering

The shop is open from the second bell of morning until the first bell of evening, except for two hours at midday. Iren receives walk-ins, but does not chat. The phrase “the seventh question of the third volume” earns the party a closed shutter, a locked door, and a kettle.

Without the phrase, the party can still commission a reading — for 25 gp per item, payable on delivery, no negotiation — but Iren will not share her own observations beyond the literal reading. With the phrase, she does.

What Iren analyses

Each item delivered is returned with a one-page handwritten analysis the next day. If the party brings multiple items at once, Iren produces a triangulated reading that is more useful than the sum of its parts — this is the keystone of the phase.

Single readings

  • Aldea Veren's reply — confirms what Aldea wrote, adds that the wax is from a recipe-family Iren has seen on one prior occasion (the Suloise hill country forgery).
  • Haskur's false papers (if recovered) — identify the forger as someone who has done this work in Greyhawk for years. Iren can name two likely forgers; one is dead; the other is reachable through Vone.
  • The Korre ledger hand — Korre imitates Aerdy legal shorthand badly. Iren is sure: she copied her training, she did not earn it. “She wrote in this hand because someone showed her how. She did not learn it from a master.”
  • “E.” marginalia (if any are shown) — the proportions are not the Korre proportions. They are not Aldea's proportions either. “This is a hand I cannot place in any tradition I know.”

The keystone reading: “Seventh Stair”

If the party brings the phrase — written on the Ashen Itinerary, on Korre's apartment notation, or simply asked — Iren goes to the locked paper press. She returns with a thin volume, dust on the gilt, and reads aloud:

“Of the route-marks set in old workings, those that descended past use were marked by their step-count: the First, the Third, the Seventh. The Seventh, where it appears, is reserved for the deeper sort, that connect to passages no longer surveyed by guild or crown. It is unwise to enter the Seventh Stair without a guide who has been there before. The author of this glossary did not return from his.”

Iren closes the book. She looks up at the party. She says:

“This is not a sea-route term. It is a descent term. I do not know what is below your Seventh Stair, but the route is older than the city. You will not find it on a port chart.”

She will not say the word Underdark. She will not name the deep organisation. She has not read these things in ink. She does not guess under her own roof.

Iren's price

Money is not the price. The price is that she wants to read everything the party recovers in Greyhawk. If the party promises to bring her copies of what they find, she gives them the readings at half rate. She means it.

Node 4 — Edrik Vone's Private Archive

High Quay, a four-storey building near the customs house. Vone occupies the second floor. The first floor is a dressmaker. The third and fourth are rented residential. The cellar is dry storage. Also see Vone's full dossier in Phase 6 — Greyhawk NPCs.

Keyed location

#RoomNotes
1ReceptionOne small room with a desk, a chair for waiting clients, and a panelled door to the archive. Vone meets clients here.
2Regular ledger archiveTwo rooms of cabinet shelves, alphabetised by client. Holds declared courier traffic for the past eight years.
3Irregular ledger archiveOne small room behind a hidden door (DC 16 to find without a guide). Holds off-book deliveries Vone never declared.
4Vone's officeTwo pens always perpendicular to a single sheet of paper. A reading lamp. A small locked cashbox.
5Back service doorOnto a narrow alley that runs to the customs house. Vone uses it when the front is being watched.

Access

The party knocks. Vone receives them in reception. He listens. He sets a price. He does not negotiate in the same conversation he sets the price; he expects the party to come back with the money or not.

Access costs

LedgerBase priceModifier (Lie 1)Modifier (Path C)Yeomanry papers shown
Regular50 gp–25 gp+50 gp+100 gp or refusal
Irregular200 gp–50 gp+100 gp+100 gp or refusal

Lie 1 in Phase 5 (the conspiracy believing the party went to Rel Astra) relaxes Greyhawk countersurveillance for 6–9 days; Vone reduces his risk markup. Path C (kinetic removal of Sereth) makes Vone nervous, and notorious clients pay more.

Other forms of leverage

  • Blackmail: a customs colleague who would prefer Vone never spoke again. Reduces price by 50%.
  • Promise of protection: Caelith's seal, an Aerdy advocate's witness slip, or a written non-action from a Greyhawk authority. Vone takes this seriously; it can substitute for half the irregular price.
  • Frightening evidence: showing Vone something that proves his archive is at risk if he does not cooperate. Reduces price by 25%; risk: Vone may flee Greyhawk by the next morning.

Clues from the regular ledger

  • Foreign-quarter courier traffic for the last six months. Many of these are routine.
  • Three packets per month on a similar timing pattern, addressed to a rotating set of Foreign Quarter rooms. The recipient initial cycles: V. K., V. Korre, K—.
  • The packets all originate from the same sender: Kestrel & Reed, Foreign Letters, on Hawkstone Way.

Clues from the irregular ledger

  • V. Korre appears in hand-altered form. The first entry is “Korre,” revised down to “V. K.” and then to “K—.” Vone's annotation reads: “repeat custom, monthly address.”
  • Korre's current monthly address is written on the back of the most recent entry. (The address is in a rented room above a bakery on Tannerway, two streets east of the Brass Crow. Korre rented it three weeks ago and has paid through the end of the month.)
  • Three Hardby-aligned packets per month from Kestrel & Reed.
  • A packet marked H.V.A. was redirected to the Brass Crow last week. Vone's annotation: “diverted by hand, see K&R counterpart entry.”
  • A pending pickup is scheduled for the eleventh bell two nights from now, at a location Vone has noted only as “old bath, Tinker's Way side.” If the Haskur Trail Clock is past 4, this pickup is cancelled.

What Vone refuses to discuss

Why these clients matter. Who Korre's principal is. What “H.V.A.” stands for. The Seventh Stair. Vone has never heard the term. He has not been paid to know it.

Node 5 — Kestrel & Reed, Foreign Letters

Hawkstone Way, six streets west of the Brass Crow. A two-storey letter agency operating as the Greyhawk office of a Hardby-based firm. The owner is in Hardby. The Greyhawk office is run by senior clerk Doras Kemmel.

Important framing. Kestrel & Reed is an intermediary, not the conspiracy itself. Staff believe they handle sensitive trade correspondence. The owner suspects some clients are dangerous but does not know the full truth. Treat them as turnable, not adversarial.

Keyed location

#RoomNotes
1Front letter counterThe public-facing room. Doras handles walk-ins. Two clerks process declarations.
2Sorting roomBehind the counter, separated by a half-door. Three sorting tables. Letters are graded by destination and priority.
3Cipher deskA small alcove off the sorting room. One clerk — junior, trustworthy — handles cipher corrections. Most cipher work is delegated to clients; this desk only does emergency decoding.
4Lockbox wallTwenty numbered iron boxes set into the wall behind the cipher desk. Each is rented to a client. Box 17 is V. Korre's; one packet has been picked up this week, one remains.
5Rear cart courtAn enclosed yard for incoming and outgoing courier carts. One gate to the alley.
6Upstairs rented roomThe owner's Greyhawk pied-à-terre. Empty most of the year. Currently empty.
7Roofline escape pathA flat tar-paper roof connecting to two adjacent buildings. Doras knows it exists; he has never used it.

Approach options

Bribe staff

Doras takes 50 gp for a single packet's metadata (sender, recipient, weight, date). He takes 200 gp for a one-time look at the lockbox wall. He prefers to be paid privately, away from the office.

Forge authorization

The party can forge a Hardby-firm authorisation to inspect a specific client's lockbox. Forgery DC 16 with proper materials; if Iren has read Korre's hand, the DC drops to 14. Detection check is rolled when Doras receives the document; he is more likely to protest than to refuse outright.

Break in

Entry is possible from the rear cart court (DC 14 lock on the gate, DC 15 on the back door) or from the roofline (DC 16 climb, DC 13 ceiling hatch). The lockbox wall itself is the difficulty: each box is DC 17 to pick, one box at a time, and the cipher desk has a small bell that rings when the wall is touched without the desk clerk's knowledge.

Shadow the pickup

The party can stake out the office and follow whoever picks up Korre's next packet. The pickup will be by Tassel or by a substitute runner, depending on the Trail Clock. Following yields the Korre apartment address without entering Kestrel & Reed at all.

Use Quill's deposition

If Vesten Quill was captured in Phase 5 and is currently giving testimony, his deposition (delivered by formal courier) gives the party legal standing to inspect Kestrel & Reed's records. Doras becomes a turnable witness within the same session. This is the cleanest entry to this node.

Yeomanry authority

Possible, but produces immediate Heat Clock advancement and warns the entire foreign-correspondent network. Korre changes address that night. Use as a last resort.

Clues recoverable from the lockbox wall

  • Quill's Loftwick packets came through here. Doras knows Quill's name (or his alias).
  • V. Korre received summaries of Caelith's office — redacted but legible enough to identify Pell as the source.
  • One packet is missing from box 17, picked up this week. The receipt says the carrier was unfamiliar; Doras describes him as “a young man in grey-and-bone clothing” without prompting.
  • One packet remains in box 17, sealed but heat-reactive. This is Handout 6.

Doras turning

If the party offers Doras formal protection — Caelith's seal, an Aerdy advocate's witness slip, or Halask if Vector D — and gives him 24 hours to think about it, he turns. He becomes a witness. His statement closes Kestrel & Reed temporarily and gives the party an open door to V. Korre's correspondence record from the past three months.

Doras has a wife and three children in the Garden Quarter. He needs to know they will be safe. The protection has to be real.

Node 6 — V. Korre's Monthly Address

The current address is a rented room above a bakery on Tannerway, two streets east of the Brass Crow. The bakery is unaware of the room's contents; the room has its own staircase from the side alley. Korre rented it three weeks ago, paid through the end of the month, and signed the lease in a hand none of the other rooms' lessees would recognise.

Reaching the address

The party reaches Korre's address by recovering it from any of the following:

  • Vone's irregular ledger (most direct).
  • Doras Kemmel's testimony, if Doras turns.
  • Shadowing Tassel from Kestrel & Reed (yields the address without intermediate paperwork).
  • Triangulation from the Brass Crow registry side mark + Iren's reading of Korre's hand + a single drop observation (Investigation DC 16, plays elegantly when the party assembles three nodes).

State of the apartment

Korre is at the apartment in one of three states, depending on the Haskur Trail Clock when the party arrives:

Trail Clock 0–2: present and packing

Korre is in the apartment with two of her agents, packing a single travelling case. She has time to flee but not to clean. The party may be able to intercept her here. This is the best-case capture scenario.

Trail Clock 3–4: absent but recently there

The hearth is warm. The kettle is empty. A burned letter strip lies in the brazier. One agent is on the stair as the party arrives; he warns Korre, who is two streets ahead. The party can search but cannot capture her here.

Trail Clock 5: bait

Korre is using the apartment to draw the party in. She has set two agents in the next room and one on the roof. If the party enters without precaution, she escapes through the bakery side and the agents engage. This is the worst case.

Clues in the apartment (regardless of state)

  • Burned letter strips in the brazier. Most are illegible. One legible fragment reads: “… clean hands drown first …” — the phrase Korre confirmed she does not understand.
  • One clean ledger line on the desk: a brief entry recording that “A.” (Andren) is to be transferred to “K.” (the courier — Kethren) at the eleventh bell, location “OBT” — Old Bath, Tinker's.
  • One wax flake on the desk, fallen from a sealing accident. The wax matches Aldea's “unknown recipe-family.” Iren can confirm.
  • Route notation: a small slip with the words “VII Stair”, struck through, then rewritten. The strike-through is in a different hand than the rewrite. Korre's hand wrote it once. The other hand — smaller, more careful — is Kethren's.
  • A charcoal sketch on the back of a folded paper: a descending stair symbol, seven steps cut under a black hill. Match this to the cistern route mark in the climax (Scene 7).
  • A second sealed packet in the desk drawer. Heat-reactive. If revealed (heat from a candle for ninety seconds), it shows: “No sail. No road. Andren to be moved before any clerk's question reaches the Watch. Korre to close. K. to follow with the second page.”

Possible social confrontation with Korre (Trail Clock 0–2)

If the party catches Korre at the apartment, she will not start a fight. She will speak. Sample lines:

  • “I am being paid to move correspondence. Nothing else.” (this is true and untrue)
  • “Andren is leaving Greyhawk in two nights. Do not stop him here.”
  • “I do not know who pays. I receive sealed instructions. The wax is always the same kind. The kind of person who can afford that kind of wax does not write their name.”
  • “The boy is not Andren's man. The boy is the route. I do not know what is at the other end.”
  • “Take me into protection and I will write what I know. Threaten me and I burn the rest.”

If the party offers genuine protection, Korre considers it. Insight DC 14 reads her tell — she is afraid, and she is tired, and she is closer to taking the offer than her face suggests. Persuasion DC 16, with concrete protection, secures her. This is the second-cleanest capture in Phase 6 (after Doras turning).

Korre's escape routes

If the party enters without precaution at Trail Clock 3+:

  • Bakery side stair down through the kitchen and out the bakery's customer door.
  • Roofline along the bakery's chimney to the next building, three buildings to the cistern's hidden entrance.
  • Service drain beneath the apartment block (this is rare, dirty, and dangerous; she uses it only if the other two are blocked).

The party should be able to cover one of these without trouble, and two with planning. Covering all three requires either Doras's help or a high-water Korre tell.
